revision.text:
[[ja:安里八幡宮]][[zh:安里八幡宮]] {{commonscat|Asato-Hachimangū|qq=}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Asato-Hachimangū‎ | image = [[File:Asato-Hachimangū.JPG|270px|Haiden and main hall]]
Haiden and main hall | location = {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|qid=Q181966|lt=Naha|1=Naha}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan, 124 Asato | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 15.9 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 41 | longitude seconds = 41.8 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[:en:Emperor Ojin|Emperor Ojin]]
{{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}
{{ill|Tamayori-hime|en|Tamayori-hime|qid=Q56344982|lt=Tamayori-hime|1=Tamayori-hime}} | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = Tenshun era (1457 - 1464) | main hall = [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | other names = | notable for = Ryukyu Eight Shrines | festival = Lunar calendar: September 9th | rituals = }} [[File:Asato-Hachimangū Gate.JPG|right|thumb|250px|Torii]] '''Asato-Hachimangū‎''' (安里八幡宮, Asato Hachimangū) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Asato|en|Asato|qid=Q721194|lt=Asato|1=Asato}}, {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|qid=Q181966|lt=Naha|1=Naha}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Meiji period]]. == Deities == The only [[Hachiman shrine]] among the Eight ShrinesJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p.23, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000, enshrining: *[[:en:Emperor Ojin|Emperor Ojin]] *{{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} *{{ill|Tamayori-hime|en|Tamayori-hime|qid=Q56344982|lt=Tamayori-hime|1=Tamayori-hime}} The other seven shrines enshrine [[Kumano Gongen]]. == History == The "Kanjō Yurai" in the "Section of Hachiman Daibosatsu" of Volume 11 of the ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom'' (1713, Kangxi 52) states that the shrine was founded during the Tenshun era (1457 - 1464). According to legend, during the reign of the 6th [[Ryukyu King]], {{ill|Shō Toku|en|Shō Toku|qid=Q907273|lt=Shō Toku|1=Shō Toku}}, soldiers were sent to subdue [[:en:Kikai Island|Kikai Island]]. An elder suggested that the king himself should lead the army for a better outcome. Upon departing, the king shot an arrow at a waterfowl, vowing that if successful, it would indicate victory. He hit the bird, and while sailing to Kikai Island, a small bell floated near the ship. The king vowed to enshrine Hachiman Daibosatsu if he succeeded in obtaining the bell, which he did. Upon his return, he built a shrine at the site where the arrow landed, enshrining the floating bell and the divine arrow as Hachiman Daibosatsu. The ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom'' also note that this story appears in the ''Ryukyu Shinto Records'' (1648), though it attributes the event to King {{ill|Shō Taikyū|en|Shō Taikyū|qid=Q1139919|lt=Shō Taikyū|1=Shō Taikyū}}. As one of the eight temples of the Shingon sect, Jindoku-ji (still located to the southwest of the shrine), the shrine was recognized as one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines under the government system. The government provided salaries and maintenance costs for shrine priests and other personnel. The clothing for rituals was also supplied by the government''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine was designated as an unranked shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. According to ''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', the Okinawa Prefectural Government planned to classify it as a village shrine, but due to economic constraints, it remained unranked''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965. The shrine was destroyed in October 1944 during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|qid=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}} and was later rebuilt. The area where the shrine is located was the battleground of the [[Battle of Sugar Loaf Hill]]. == References == {{References|qq=}} {{reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Chuchu Ryotei|title=Ryukyu Shinto Records|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|qq=}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|qq=}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|qq=}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= |qq=}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Asato-Hachimangū|qq=}} *[[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/asato.html Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office Official Site Asato-Hachimangū‎] {{Shinto side|qq=}} {{Okinawan Shrines|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|安里八幡宮|version=94116705|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:安里八幡宮]] [[zh:%E5%AE%89%E9%87%8C%E5%85%AB%E5%B9%A1%E5%AE%AE]] {{Commons category|Asato-Hachimangū}} [[zh:安里八幡宮]] {{Commons category|Asato-Hachimangū}} [[zh:安里八幡宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11451643}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:沖縄県の旧無格社]] [[Category:那覇市の歴史]] [[Category:incorrect template category applied]]
